content.text: Retrofit 2.6 added first-class coroutine support — declaring a service method as
is all it takes to get a non-blocking, coroutine-friendly HTTP call with no custom

required. Claude's Messages API is a straightforward POST endpoint that returns a complete JSON response, making it a perfect fit for this pattern. You define the interface, add Gson for JSON conversion, inject the service into a Repository, and call it from a
— all within the structured concurrency lifecycle Android Compose already expects.

attrs.alt: Retrofit suspend adapter architecture: Compose UI → ChatViewModel → ClaudeRepository → ClaudeApiService → Claude API, with data model flow
attrs.title: Retrofit Suspend Architecture for Claude Messages API

content.text: Retrofit's coroutine support is built into the core library since 2.6.0 — no separate coroutines adapter artifact is needed. Add Gson for JSON serialization and OkHttp for the underlying HTTP layer:
Define Kotlin data classes that mirror Claude's Messages API JSON schema. Gson's
maps snake_case API fields to camelCase Kotlin properties:
methods as first-class citizens. Internally, Retrofit wraps them in a
call adapter — you don't write a custom
to access HTTP status codes and headers, or plain

Never embed the API key in the app binary. In production, route all requests through a backend proxy that reads the key from a server-side environment variable and accepts session tokens from your app.
instance at app startup and reuse it. Use Hilt or a simple object singleton. The OkHttp client adds read/write timeouts and an optional logging interceptor for development:

import okhttp3.OkHttpClient
import okhttp3.logging.HttpLoggingInterceptor
import retrofit2.Retrofit
import retrofit2.converter.gson.GsonConverterFactory
import java.util.concurrent.TimeUnit

object NetworkModule {

    private val loggingInterceptor = HttpLoggingInterceptor().apply {
        level = if (BuildConfig.DEBUG)
            HttpLoggingInterceptor.Level.BODY
        else
            HttpLoggingInterceptor.Level.NONE
    }

    private val okHttpClient = OkHttpClient.Builder()
        .addInterceptor(loggingInterceptor)
        .connectTimeout(30, TimeUnit.SECONDS)
        .readTimeout(120, TimeUnit.SECONDS)  // Claude can take ~60 s on long requests
        .writeTimeout(30, TimeUnit.SECONDS)
        .build()

    val claudeApiService: ClaudeApiService = Retrofit.Builder()
        .baseUrl("https://api.anthropic.com/")   // trailing slash required
        .client(okHttpClient)
        .addConverterFactory(GsonConverterFactory.create())
        .build()
        .create(ClaudeApiService::class.java)
}
The Repository owns all HTTP interaction: it constructs the
, calls the suspend service method, and converts HTTP errors into domain-typed results. Using
avoids leaking Retrofit exceptions into the ViewModel:
catches all exceptions — including those thrown by Retrofit's JSON deserialization — and wraps them in a
block normalises network vs HTTP errors so the ViewModel receives a uniform
The ViewModel owns the UI state and coroutine scope. It holds the conversation history so multi-turn conversations work correctly, and exposes a single
(lifecycle-aware, stops collecting when the screen is not in the foreground) and renders a loading indicator, message list, or error banner accordingly:
Three distinct failure categories require different handling strategies:
Non-2xx when using plain T return type
Parse error body; 429 → retry after X seconds; 401 → re-auth
Malformed JSON — usually an intermediate proxy
Log raw response body; report to crash tracker
Must be rethrown — do not catch in Repository
Parsing the 4xx error body requires a second Gson call because Retrofit's
lets you enqueue real HTTP responses and verify the Repository handles them correctly without any network access:
Claude can take 30–60 seconds for long completions. OkHttp's default read timeout is 10 seconds. Set it to at least 90–120 seconds.
in a coroutine swallows cancellation. Always rethrow
exhausts the underlying stream. Read the error body once, store the string, then parse it.
must end with a slash. Without it, a relative path like
replaces the last path segment instead of appending, producing the wrong URL.
Mixing multi-turn history with streaming endpoints.

When using suspend adapters for non-streaming requests, history accumulation is straightforward. For streaming (SSE), history must be serialized differently — the two approaches should use separate service methods.
or string resources are extractable from the APK. Always route requests through a backend proxy where the key lives server-side.
Response<T> vs Plain T: Which to Choose
Retrofit's suspend integration supports two return-type forms for each service method:
Simple callers that just want the body or an exception
Any caller that needs headers, error body, or HTTP code
in the Repository so you can parse the 4xx error body (which contains Anthropic's structured error JSON) before surfacing the message to the user.
Declare the service return type as
to access error bodies and inspect HTTP status; use plain
so the ViewModel receives a uniform type regardless of failure mode.
Set the OkHttp read timeout to at least 90 seconds — Claude can take longer than the 10-second default on complex requests.
to unit-test all HTTP scenarios — success, 4xx with error body, network timeout — without any real network.
Never embed the API key in the APK. Route all requests through a backend proxy that holds the key server-side.
